mainmatter.p: I finished my pass through towards a geometry for syntax and submitted the revision to the arXiv.
Today I added an experimental \iftex command to Forester 2.5 [unreleased] in response to Vikraman Choudhury’s post to the mailing list.

I started to resume my work on strong impredicative encodings in intensional type theory with Mike Shulman in terms of wild category theory. I ended up getting totally snowed under coherences and asked Mike Shulman for help.
My new spaces for denotational semantics grant was awarded by United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research.

In regard to strong impredicative encodings, Mike Shulman answered my question from yesterday: apparently we can nonetheless split arbitrary quasi-idempotents in the wild category of A-algebras, and this makes essential use of the second component of the homotopy \mathsf{happly} \, Q_0 \colon m \circ m \sim m. It is really fascinating that the splitting that I constructed last time worked differently and seemed to take advantage of the specific cone involved; this clarity is a really nice consequence of Mike’s preference to focus on the more general result.

Today I finished my Agda formalization in TypeTopology of the wild-categorical results that Mike Shulman and I were hoping for in regard to strong impredicative encodings. Then I translated these proofs into English. I started trying to re-deduce impredicative subuniverses are reflective in this style, but noticed that there may be an issue: in the old proof, we split a very specific quasi-idempotent, but here I am trying to split them all. I wrote to Mike Shulman mentioning this to him.

Mike Shulman has sent me a potential proof of quasi-idempotence for the generic element of a semicoherent cone. This relies on a “fattened” definition of semicoherence in the wild-categorical setting, similar to Mike’s fattened version in the special case of subuniverses. Mike points out that the condition, when specialized for cones over the identity functor, is much simpler than the general case. I am thinking that I might see if it is possible to formalize it in TypeTopology.

I have added an informal job posting to my home page in regard to the newly awarded new spaces for denotational semantics grant. I have also sent my final revision of towards a geometry for syntax to the editor of the Grothendieck Conference proceedings.
I received all my new office equipment and have mostly set it up; will bring a mechanical keyboard from home tomorrow. I am using the new equipment to give my topos colloquium.
I finished my pass through towards a geometry for syntax and submitted the revision to the arXiv.
Today I added an experimental \iftex command to Forester 2.5 [unreleased] in response to Vikraman Choudhury’s post to the mailing list.

I started to resume my work on strong impredicative encodings in intensional type theory with Mike Shulman in terms of wild category theory. I ended up getting totally snowed under coherences and asked Mike Shulman for help.
My new spaces for denotational semantics grant was awarded by United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research.
I received from Huang Xu a very interesting expository manuscript about synthetic Tait computability. Today I have been studying Weber’s paper on Yoneda structures from 2-toposes.

A 2-categorical generalisation of the notion of elementary topos is provided, and some of the properties of the Yoneda structure (Street and Walters, J. Algebra, 50:350–379, 1978) it generates are explored. Results enabling one to exhibit objects as cocomplete in the sense definable within a Yoneda structure are presented. Examples relevant to the globular approach to higher dimensional category theory are discussed. This paper also contains some expository material on the theory of fibrations internal to a finitely complete 2-category (Street, Lecture Notes in Math., 420:104–133, 1974) and provides a self-contained development of the necessary background material on Yoneda structures.

It is tough going so far, but the paper is very helpfully expository. I found it a little grating that it refers to comma objects as “lax pullbacks”, when these are not actually the lax limits of a given cospan.

frontmatter.title: Hofmann–Streicher lifting and comonadicity of fibrations

mainmatter.p: Yesterday, I happened upon the video of Guiseppe Rosolini’s talk at the Grothendieck Conference, and I finally got around to looking a preprint that it refers to:

We study the 2-category theory of Grothendieck fibrations in the 2-category of functors \mathbf{Cat} ^{ \to }. After redrawing a few general results in that context, we show that fibrations over a given base are pseudo-coalgebras for a 2-comonad on { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B }. We use that result to explain how an arbitrary fibration is equivalent to one with a splitting.

What the paper proves is that in addition to fibrations being 2-monadic over displayed categories, they are also 2-comonadic. I noticed that the Hofmann–Streicher lifting actually factors through this additional right 2-adjoint. This sounds like a promising path forward for my own attempts to generalize Hofmann–Streicher lifting to a 2-functor over an arbitrary 2-category.

I also spent some time preparing the final revision of towards a geometry for syntax, and wrangling our Research Office to respond to United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research regarding new spaces for denotational semantics.

I received a brief and positive referee report my contribution towards a geometry for syntax to the post-proceedings of the Grothendieck Conference, which raised an interesting question: to what degree was Per Martin-Löf in fact aware of Grothendieck’s universes? In any case, I think I should be a bit more precise about the attributions here, and naturally it would be appropriate to mention Zermelo’s priority as well. I did fix the typographical error found by Marcelo Fiore.

I met with Marcelo Fiore, and we realized something very interesting. The essential image of { \mathbf{Set} } \xrightarrow{\Delta} { \mathbf{Set} ^{ \mathbb{F} } } appears to be the orthogonal subcategory corresponding to { \left \{ I \times \mathbb{V} \to I \mid I \right \} }; but this means that the full subcategory spanned by constant objects extends to a reflective subfibration. Our idea was to use this as part of our axiomatization of constant or discrete objects in \mathbf{Set} ^ \mathbb{F}, but it occurred to me that the same might work in the topos of forests to access constant objects without a comonad — by considering orthogonality to \rhd \bot. At first this seemed promising, but now I see that the reflection for this orthogonality class must certainly take \rhd{X} to the point, and so it would delete everything except the head of a guarded stream. Alas.
Forester 2.4 is now released on opam. Lîm Tsú-thuàn is interested in implementing a forester watch command, as we have discussed on the mailing list.
Today I am working on releasing Forester 2.4 to opam.
Karl Crary announced that I won the 2022 Ed Clarke Dissertation Award.

I have been thinking about monoidal closed structures induced by slicing over a monoid, which has been considered by Combette and Munch-Maccagnoni as a potential denotational semantics of destructors à la C++. It occurred to me that this construction is, in fact, almost a degenerate case of Day convolution on an internal monoidal category — and this made me realize that there might be a nice way to understand Day convolution in the language of fibered categories. In fact, many of these results (in particular the relativization to internal monoidal categories) are probably a special case of Theorem 11.22 of Shulman’s paper on enriched indexed categories.

Let E and F be two displayed categories over a semimonoidal category { \left ( B , \otimes , \alpha \right )}. We may define the “Day tensor” product E \otimes _ B F of E and F to be the following displayed category over B:
mainmatter.tex: E \otimes _ B F : \equiv \otimes _! { \left ( \pi _1^* E \times _{ B \times B } \pi _2^* F \right )}

frontmatter.title: Stepping through the Day tensor of displayed categories

mainmatter.p: To understand the construction of the Day tensor, we will go through it step-by-step. Let E and F be two displayed categories over a semimonoidal category { \left ( B , \otimes , \alpha \right )}.

ol.li: Given that E and F are displayed over B, we may restrict them to lie the two disjoint regions of B \times B by base change along the two projections: The above can be seen as cartesian lift in the 2-bifibration of displayed categories over \mathbf{Cat}.

Next, we took the fiber product of displayed categories, giving a vertical span over B: \pi _1^* E \leftarrow \pi _1^* E \times _{ B \times B } \pi _2^* F \rightarrow \pi _2^* F Of course, this corresponds to pullback in \mathbf{Cat} or cartesian product in { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B }.

Finally, we take a cocartesian lift along the tensor functor { B \times B } \xrightarrow{\otimes} { B } to obtain the Day tensor: The cocartesian lift above corresponds to composition in \mathbf{Cat}.

mainmatter.p: Under appropriate assumptions, we may also compute a “Day hom” by adjointness.

Let E and F be two displayed categories over a semimonoidal category { \left ( B , \otimes , \alpha \right )} such that E is a cartesian fibration. We may define the “Day hom” E \multimap _ B F of E and F to be the following displayed category over B:
mainmatter.tex: E \multimap _ B F : \equiv { \left ( \pi _1 \right )} _* { \left ( \pi _2^* E \Rightarrow _{ B \times B } \otimes ^* F \right )}

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: First of all, we note that the pullback functor { { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B } } \xrightarrow{\pi _1^*} { { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B \times B } } has a right adjoint \pi _1^* \dashv { \left ( \pi _1 \right )} _*, as { B \times B } \xrightarrow{\pi _1} { B } is (as any cartesian fibration) a Conduché functor, i.e. an exponentiable arrow in \mathbf{Cat}. We have already assumed that E is a Cartesian fibrations, and thus so is its restriction \pi _1^*E; it therefore follows that \pi _1^*E is exponentiable. With that out of the way, we may compute the hom by adjoint calisthenics:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} & \mathbf{hom} _{ { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B } } { \left ( X \otimes _B E , F \right )} \\ & \quad \equiv \mathbf{hom} _{ { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B } } { \left ( \otimes _! { \left ( \pi _1^*X \times _{B \times B} \pi _2^* E \right )} , F \right )} \\ & \quad \simeq \mathbf{hom} _{ { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B \times B } } { \left ( \pi _1^*X \times _{B \times B} \pi _2^* E , \otimes ^* F \right )} \\ & \quad \simeq \mathbf{hom} _{ { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B \times B } } { \left ( \pi _1^*X , \pi _2^* E \Rightarrow \otimes ^* F \right )} \\ & \quad \simeq \mathbf{hom} _{ { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B } } { \left ( X , { \left ( \pi _1 \right )} _* { \left ( \pi _2^* E \Rightarrow \otimes ^* F \right )} \right )} \end{aligned}

mainmatter.p: Let { \left ( B , \otimes ,I, \alpha , \lambda , \rho \right )} be a monoidal category. We may define a “Day unit” for displayed categories over B to be given by the discrete fibration { B } _{ / I } \to B, which corresponds under the Grothendieck construction to the presheaf represented by I.

frontmatter.title: The Day tensor preserves cartesian fibrations

mainmatter.p: If E and F are cartesian fibrations over a semimonoidal category { \left ( B , \otimes , \alpha \right )}, then the Day tensor E \otimes _ B F is also a cartesian fibration.

I believe, but did not check carefully, that when E and F are discrete fibrations over a semimonoidal category { \left ( B , \otimes , \alpha \right )} then the Day tensor is precisely the discrete fibration corresponding to the (contravariant) Day convolution of the presheaves corresponding to E and F. Likewise when { \left ( B , \otimes ,I, \alpha , \lambda , \rho \right )} is monoidal, it appears that the Day unit corresponds precisely to the traditional one.

There remain some interesting directions to explore. First of all, the claims above would obviously lead to a new construction of the Day convolution monoidal structure on the 1-category of discrete fibrations on B that coincides with the traditional one up to the Grothendieck construction. But in general, we should expect to exhibit both { \mathbf{Cat} } _{ / B } and \mathbf{Fib} _{ B } as monoidal bicategories, a result that I have not seen before.

frontmatter.title: A monoidal bicategory of displayed categories

mainmatter.p: Let { \left ( B , \otimes ,I, \alpha , \lambda , \rho \right )} be a monoidal category. Then the Day tensor and unit extend to a monoidal structure on the bicategory of displayed categories over B.

The conjecture above is highly non-trivial, as monoidal bicategories are extremely difficult to construct explicitly. I am hoping that Mike Shulman’s ideas involving monoidal double categories could potentially help.

I met with Marcelo Fiore and taught him the equivalence between Artin gluing and open/closed modalities. I also began to prepare my FAQ about starting a PhD with me, and got some useful feedback from people on Mastodon.

frontmatter.title: On the relationship between QTT and STC

mainmatter.p: I have been thinking again about the relationship between quantitative type theory and synthetic Tait computability and other approaches to type refinements. One of the defining characteristics of QTT that I thought distinguished it from STC was the treatment of types: in QTT, types only depend on the “computational” / unrefined aspect of their context, whereas types in STC are allowed to depend on everything. In the past, I mistakenly believed that this was due to the realizability-style interpretation of QTT, in contrast with STC’s gluing interpretation. It is now clear to me that (1) QTT is actually glued (in the sense of q-realizability, no pun intended), and (2) the nonstandard interpretation of types in QTT corresponds to adding an additional axiom to STC, namely the tininess of the generic proposition.

It has been suggested to me by Neel Krishnaswami that this property of QTT may not be desirable in all cases (sometimes you want the types to depend on quantitative information), and that for this reason, graded type theories might be a better way forward in some applications. My results today show that STC is, in essence, what you get when you relax the QTT’s assumption that types do not depend on quantitative information. This suggests that we should explore the idea of multiplicities within the context of STC — as any monoidal product on the subuniverse spanned by closed-modal types induces quite directly a form of variable multiplicity in STC, I expect this direction to be fruitful.

My thoughts on the precise relationship between the QTT models and Artin gluing will be elucidated at a different time. Today, I will restrict myself to sketching an interpretation of a QTT-style language in STC assuming the generic proposition is internally tiny.

Let \mathscr{Q} be an elementary topos equipped with a subterminal object \P \hookrightarrow \mathbf{1} inducing an open subtopos \mathscr{E} \simeq { \mathscr{Q} } _{ / \P } \hookrightarrow \mathscr{Q} and its complementary closed subtopos \mathscr{F} \hookrightarrow \mathscr{Q}. This structure is the basis of the interpretation of STC; if you think of STC in terms of refinements, then stuff from \mathscr{E} is “computational” and stuff from \mathscr{F} is “logical”.

We now consider the interpretation of a language of (potentially quantitative) refinements into \mathscr{Q}. A context \Gamma is interpreted by an object of \mathscr{Q}; a type \Gamma \vdash A is interpreted by a family A \to \bigcirc{\Gamma}; a term \Gamma \vdash a : A is interpreted as a map \Gamma \to A such that \Gamma \to A \to \bigcirc \Gamma is the unit of the monad.

So far we have not needed anything beyond the base structure of STC in order to give an interpretation of types in QTT’s style. But to extend this interpretation to a universe, we must additionally assume that \P is internally tiny, in the sense that the exponential functor { \left ( - \right )} ^ \P is a left adjoint. Under these circumstances, the idempotent monad \bigcirc \equiv j_*j^* : \mathscr{Q} \to \mathscr{Q} corresponding to the open immersion j : \mathscr{E} \hookrightarrow \mathscr{Q} has a right adjoint \square : \mathscr{Q} \to \mathscr{Q}, an idempotent comonad.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: As \bigcirc \equiv j_*j^* is the exponential functor { \left ( - \right )} ^ \P, its right adjoint \square is therefore the “root functor” { \left ( - \right )} _ \P that exhibits \P as an internally tiny object.

mainmatter.p: Although \square lifts to each slice of \mathscr{Q}, these liftings do not commute with base change; this will, however, not be an obstacle for us.

We will now see how to use the adjunction \bigcirc \dashv \square to interpret a universe, either for the purpose of interpreting universes of refinement types, or for the purpose of strictifying the model that we have sketched. Let \mathcal{V} be a (standard) universe in \mathscr{Q}, e.g. a Hofmann–Streicher universe; we shall then interpret the corresponding universe of refinements as \mathcal{U} : \equiv \square \mathcal{V}. To see that \mathcal{U} classifies \mathcal{V}-small families of refinements, we compute as follows:

ol.li: A code \Gamma \vdash \hat{A} : \mathcal{U} amounts to nothing more than a morphism \hat{A} : \Gamma \to \square{\mathcal{V}}.
By adjoint transpose, this is the same as a morphism \hat{A} ^ \sharp : \bigcirc{\Gamma} \to \mathcal{V}.

mainmatter.p: Thus we see that if \mathcal{V} is generic for \mathcal{V}-small families of (arbitrary) types in \mathscr{Q}, then \mathcal{U} \equiv \square \mathcal{V} is generic for \mathcal{V}-small families of type refinements, i.e. types whose context is \bigcirc-modal.
Finally, we comment that the tininess of \P is satisfied in many standard examples, the simplest of which is the Sierpiński topos \mathbf{Set} ^{ \to }.
Today I am attending Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32.

frontmatter.title: Jack Romo on homotopy bicategories of complete 2-fold Segal spaces

meta.name: venue

link.title: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

meta.link: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

mainmatter.p: Jack Romo gave a talk about homotopy bicategories of complete 2-fold Segal spaces. The goal of the work is to convert (∞,2)-categories to their (homotopy) bicategories; he has managed to define the homotopy bicategory, but I believe it is not yet functorial (part of the problem is to find the appropriate “category” for the domain of this “functor”).

The model of (∞,2)-category used is complete 2-fold Segal space, which is like an iteration of the Segal conditions. A complete Segal space is a simplicial diagram of (∞,1)-groupoids satisfying certain lifting conditions — the Segal condition says the projection of n-simplices to their “spines” should be a trivial fibration (contractible fibers!) A complete 2-fold Segal space is a simplicial diagram of complete Segal spaces satisfying similar conditions.

Computing the homotopy bicategory is non-trivial as various properties (existence of certainly lifts in the complete 2-fold Segal space) must be converted into structure (explicit associators, etc.). Jack is using an unbiased definition of bicategory due to Tom Leinster that is known to be equivalent to the bicategories of Jean Bénabou.

frontmatter.title: Stefania Damato on a container model of type theory

meta.name: venue

link.title: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

meta.link: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

mainmatter.p: Stefania Damato describes a model of type theory in (generalized) containers, joint work with her PhD supervisor Thorsten Altenkirch. The background of this work is the prior functorial semantics of QIITs due to Thorsten Altenkirch and Ambrus Kaposi.

In that prior work, the universal property of QIITs is described in terms of certain functors having initial algebras, but no conditions were given to ensure that these initial algebras exist. In the context of ordinary inductive types, presentation by a container is a sufficient notion (i.e. the functor should be polynomial). In this work, Stefania Damato and Thorsten Altenkirch have needed to generalize the notion of polynomial in a way that Nicola Gambino suggested is equivalent to familial representability.

The motivation for finding a model of type theory in containers is that this is apparently necessary in order to be able to use type theoretic constructs in the signature of a QIIT. I never did fully understand how this relates to the constructs that appear in the constructors of the QIIT once its initial algebra is computed, as these happen in different models of type theory: the QIIT is specified, apparently, in the container model but then the QIIT is used in (e.g.) the \mathbf{Set} model. I chatted with Stefania Damato afterward and she offered some helpful answers, but I don’t think I fully understood this point.

meta.name: venue

link.title: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

meta.link: Yorkshire and Midlands Category Theory Seminar #32

mainmatter.p: Kristina Sojakova described a huge advance on her prior work on syllepsis in homotopy type theory. In the prior work, there is a lot of path algebra to establish the syllepsis — to the point where going any further is practically impossible. Kristina has come up with a new way to prove Eckmann–Hilton, syllepsis, and higher syllepses that completely avoids this path algebra.

The idea is to generalize each statement to get rid of all the loops; in other words, a loop x : A, p: x = x is replaced by x : A, y : A, p : x = y as well as whatever other things are needed in order to generalize the statement involving loops. This doesn’t appear to be completely mechanical, but it is at least methodical. Once all the loops have been removed, the generalized statement is proved by applying path induction a ton of times, mindlessly. Then the original statement involving loops is easily seen to be an instance of the generalized one.
The new argument scales effortlessly from Eckmann–Hilton to syllepsis, to the “syllepsis of the syllepsis”.

This was easily the most interesting talk of YaMCATS#32 for me; I am wondering if there are more painful things in HoTT that can be dealt with in this way. Jamie Vicary asked whether the proof is depending heavily on path induction having definitional computation rules; I think this is indeed the crux of the proof, although Benno van den Berg mentioned that it might still be possible to use this proof technique with the weak version of path induction. I think this is within the realm of “possibility” but I think it would be very painful.

Following up on my meeting yesterday with Marcelo Fiore, I have been trying to understand what is happening with the exponential in the gluing of { \mathbf{Set} ^ \mathbb{N} } \xrightarrow{e_*} { \mathbf{Set} ^ \mathbb{F} }, i.e. the direct image of the essential geometric morphism induced by the discrete inclusion \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{F}. As it happens, I believe we had been considering the exponential from the family \eta _A : A \to e_*e^*A to the family \eta _B : B \to e_*e^*B and we were startled to see that the \mathbf{Set} ^ \mathbb{F} component of this family seemed to the exponential B^A itself.

We conjectured at the time that this might have something to do with the surjectivity of N \to \mathbb{F} on objects or some related property, e.g. the induced geometric morphism being a surjection of topoi. However, I have now shown that this always when you glue topoi along a functor that has a left adjoint.

frontmatter.title: Exponentials of the form i_!A \Rightarrow i_!B

mainmatter.p: Let \mathscr{C} and \mathscr{D} be cartesian closed categories, and let \rho ^* \dashv \rho _*: \mathscr{C} \to \mathscr{D} be an adjunction. Define \mathscr{G} to be the Artin gluing of the right adjoint { \mathscr{C} } \xrightarrow{\rho _*} { \mathscr{D} }. Involving \mathscr{C}, we have an adjunction j^* \dashv j_*: \mathscr{C} \hookrightarrow \mathscr{G}; involving \mathscr{D}, we have an adjoint triple i_! \dashv i^* \dashv i_*: \mathscr{D} \hookrightarrow \mathscr{G}. We have canonical isomorphism i^* { \left ( {i_!B}^{i_!A} \right )} \cong B^A.

block.headline: Proof (Sketch).
block.p: We describe the actions of all these functors on objects below:

We recall the standard computation of the glued exponential i_!A \Rightarrow {i_!B} as the pulled back family below, in which the lower left map is defined using the fact that \rho _* preserves binary products:
We may simplify the above using our explicit computations of all the adjoints, noting that i^*i_! = 1 and j^*i_! = \rho ^*:
It is possible to construct a likely inverse to the lower composite. In particular, we will show that the following natural isomorphism is given by postcomposition with h:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} { \left [ X, \rho _* { \left ( \rho ^*A \Rightarrow \rho ^*B \right )} \right ]} & \cong { \left [ \rho ^*X, \rho ^*A \to \rho ^*B \right ]} \\ & \cong { \left [ \rho ^*X \times \rho ^*A, \rho ^*B \right ]} \\ & \cong { \left [ \rho ^* { \left ( X \times A \right )} , \rho ^*B \right ]} \\ & \cong { \left [ X \times A, \rho _* \rho ^*B \right ]} \\ & \cong { \left [ X,A \Rightarrow \rho _* \rho ^*B \right ]} \end{aligned}
block.p: (I didn't have time to finish writing that proof. Maybe there is a better way that doesn’t involve this awful computation? I believe that Marcelo Fiore has independently proved it this afternoon.)

mainmatter.p: In our meeting yesterday, Marcelo pointed out that the generic object V of the object classifier has a very interesting property: the map V+1 \to V^V sending the left side to the constant map and the right side to the identity map is an isomorphism. I conjectured that this can be deduced from synthetic quasicoherence in the sense of Blechschmidt, which is a generalization of the Kock–Lawvere axiom from synthetic differential geometry to arbitrary geometric theories. I have now stated and proved this statement precisely. First we recall the synthetic quasicoherence theorem of Blechschmidt:

Let \mathbb{T} be a geometric theory and let U_{ \mathbb{T} } be its generic model in \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathbb{T} \right ]}. The spectrum of a U_{ \mathbb{T} }-algebra M is the set of U_{ \mathbb{T} }-algebra homomorphisms M \to U_{ \mathbb{T} }. (Recall that a U_{ \mathbb{T} }-algebra is a model of \mathbb{T} equipped with a homomorphism from U_{ \mathbb{T} }.)

Let \mathbb{T} be a horn theory. The generic \mathbb{T}-model U_{ \mathbb{T} } is quasicoherent in the following sense: internal to \mathbf{Set} { \left [ \mathbb{T} \right ]}, for any finitely presentable U_{ \mathbb{T} }-algebra A, the canonical U_{ \mathbb{T} }-algebra homomorphism A \to U_{ \mathbb{T} } ^{ \operatorname{Spec} {A}} sending a to \lambda f: \operatorname{Spec} {A}. f \, a is an isomorphism, where \operatorname{Spec} {A} is the synthetic spectrum of A.
From the above, the desired result follows immediately.

frontmatter.title: Synthetic quasicoherence in the object classifier

mainmatter.p: Let V be the generic object in the object classifier \mathbf{Set} { \left [ V \right ]}. Then the map V+1 \to V^V mapping \mathsf{inl} \, x to \lambda \_ .x and \mathsf{inr} \, { \left ( \right )} to \lambda x.x is an isomorphism.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: We consider the finitely presentable V-algebra V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]}, i.e. the free extension of V by an element \mathsf{x}. By synthetic quasicoherence, the canonical homomorphism V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} \to V^{ \operatorname{Spec} { V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} }} is an isomorphism. We will see that this homomorphism is, in fact, tracked (up to isomorphism) by the desired map V+1 \to V^V.

First of all, the carrier of V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} is the coproduct V+1. The spectrum of V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} can be seen to be V itself by the universal property of the free extension:

The canonical isomorphism above sends a homomorphism { \operatorname{Spec} { V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} } } \xrightarrow{f} { V } to the element f \mathsf{x}. We therefore have the desired isomorphism V+1 \cong V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} \cong V^{ \operatorname{Spec} { V { \left [ \mathsf{x} \right ]} }} \cong V^V.

frontmatter.title: The Phoa axiom and synthetic quasicoherence

mainmatter.p: Can the Phoa axiom in synthetic domain theory be deduced from synthetic quasicoherence? I believe that Paul Taylor may have commented on something in this direction once in MathOverflow, but I do not know if anyone has proved this. I think it could be an interesting test-case. In cases where we have a clean understanding of the geometric theory in question (as in simplicial sets), this might be feasible; but I wonder about more sophisticated models of synthetic domain theory.

frontmatter.title: 2023-09-12 / Marcelo Fiore / computing with abstract syntax

mainmatter.p: Met with Marcelo to discuss the relationship between \mathbf{Set} ^N and \mathbf{Set} ^F where F is the category of finite sets and injections and N is the set of natural numbers. Marcelo points out that the base change adjoint triple induced by N \to F gives monads and comonads on \mathbf{Set} ^F for which the latter is both the topos of algebras and the topos of coalgebras.

These modal operators are used to describe the “computational shadows” of operations involving abstract syntax, as in the work of Fiore and Szamozvancev. Marcelo and I are interested in understanding how to formally show that these computational shadows are in fact correct / lie in the image of the correct operations within \mathbf{Set} ^F.

We began to conjecture that you can glue along the direct image { \mathbf{Set} ^N } \xrightarrow{i _{ * }} { \mathbf{Set} ^F } to get a certification of the algorithms as a kind of logical relation. One thing that we noticed is that, surprisingly, it looks almost as if computational aspect of the exponential in the glued topos adds almost no information whatsoever; the statement of this feeling is not yet precise. However, it should be noted the geometric morphism i : \hat{N} \to \hat{F} is a surjection, and it may be that when gluing along a surjection something interesting happens.

Another thing I noticed is that the nLab says that every geometric surjection arises as the cofree algebra functor from for a lex comonad, and vice versa. So there may be something very important happening here in relation to the theory of monadic descent, etc.
I am trying to learn more properly the theory of 2-monads and pseudomonads, in order to better understand the ideas of Marcelo Fiore and Glynn Winskel. I am studying the following paper:

frontmatter.title: Relative pseudomonads, Kleisli bicategories, and substitution monoidal structures
frontmatter.meta: @article{fiore-gambino-hyland-winskel-2018, author = {Fiore, M. and Gambino, N. and Hyland, M. and Winskel, G.}, doi = {10.1007/s00029-017-0361-3}, isbn = {1420-9020}, journal = {Selecta Mathematica}, number = {3}, pages = {2791--2830}, title = {Relative pseudomonads, Kleisli bicategories, and substitution monoidal structures}, volume = {24}, year = {2018}, }

mainmatter.p: We introduce the notion of a relative pseudomonad, which generalizes the notion of a pseudomonad, and define the Kleisli bicategory associated to a relative pseudomonad. We then present an efficient method to define pseudomonads on the Kleisli bicategory of a relative pseudomonad. The results are applied to define several pseudomonads on the bicategory of profunctors in an homogeneous way and provide a uniform approach to the definition of bicategories that are of interest in operad theory, mathematical logic, and theoretical computer science.

One thing that initially confused me was the comment in the above that for a pseudomonad T on 2-category, the Kleisli construction produces only a bicategory. The reason this was confusing to me is that I was thinking of \mathbf{Prof} as the 2-category of presheaf categories and cocontinuous maps, but I realized this is only biequivalent to the appropriate (relative) Kleisli bicategory. Indeed, every bicategory is biequivalent to a 2-category.

It appears that Chapter 5 of Fosco’s coend calculus book should be helpful for me to study. Unfortunately, op. cit. uses the opposite convention for 1-cells in \mathbf{Prof} than I am used to, saying that an arrow \mathfrak{p} : \mathcal{A} \leadsto \mathcal{B} is defined to be a functor \mathcal{A} ^{ \mathsf{o} } \times \mathcal{B} \to \mathbf{Set}, whereas Fiore et al. describe it in the other way. The latter corresponds correctly with the Kleisli bicategory viewpoint. Of course, this discrepency also impacts the definition of composition of profunctors in a deeply confusing way.

I asked Fosco about this, and he says the reason for his convention is that it is confusing if a profunctor is covariant in its “domain” and contravariant in its “codomain”. This of course makes senes, but I will have to think about the implications.

I discussed with Marcelo the definition of single-variable substitution in the object classifier using the tininess of the interval. I plan to try and define this in Agda-flat; I am curious if I can get it to compute, using rewrite rules for the “amazing” transpose.
We also discussed the plan for the Discrete Mathematics course. My lectures should start somewhere in the set theory section, and then continue through regular languages and automata to the end.

Following my meeting with Marcelo, I started to resurrect the old Agda-flat code for tininess from LOPS using the present-day --cohesion option. I managed to define the substitution function and it does pass the termination checker, but it doesn’t compute in Agda (even using --rewriting.)
Thanks to Lîm Tsú-thuàn and Vikraman Choudhury for their suggestions and bug reports on the mailing list.

ul.li: A new \iftex command has been added, following a suggestion of Vikraman Choudhury; this can be used to control the behavior of macros, depending on whether they are being rendered by \LaTeX or by the browser. For example, \iftex{foo}{bar} will become foo when passed to the \LaTeX tool, but will be reated as bar when rendering for the browser.

meta.name: venue

link.title: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

meta.link: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

link.title: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

td.link: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

mainmatter.p: What does it mean for a piece of software to be correct? There are many possible degrees and dimensions of correctness, e.g. safety, functional correctness, computational complexity, and security. To grapple with this diversity of verification requirements, semanticists develop mathematical models of program behavior that put into relief different aspects of the physical reality of program execution on hardware, just as physicists create many idealized mathematical models to study different aspects of the material reality of the universe.

Mathematical models enable us to reason about program behavior by viewing highly complex objects as being glued together from smaller, simpler objects that are easier to study in isolation. For instance, operational models aim to reduce the behavior of a process to that of individual steps of discrete computation that take place on an idealized computer; in contrast, denotational models reduce the complex global behavior of a process to the simpler local behavior of its constituent subroutines. One advantage of operational methods is that they are applicable even in situations that challenge the modularity of denotational semantics, e.g. where it is not yet understood how to reduce the global behavior of a program to that of its components. On the other hand, denotational methods provide vastly stronger and simpler reasoning principles for program verification when available.

The central thesis of denotational semantics is that programs arrange themselves into geometrical spaces called computational domains, and that a computational process can be thought of as the limit of a sequence of continuous transformations on these domains. Although this thesis has been amply born out for simple kinds of program, today’s most urgent verification requirements pertain to program constructs like concurrency and side effects whose treatment requires the introduction of new kinds of space: for instance, the correct treatment of branching behavior for concurrent processes requires the introduction of higher-dimensional computational domains in which programs can “remember” the specific way that they were glued together.

This project will extend the reach of denotational semantics and its attendant advantages for program verification into terrains where scientists have historically struggled to enact the reduction of global behavior to local behavior, making essential use of new advances in the homotopical and geometrical understanding of computation via higher dimensional category theory and topos theory. I will investigate two areas that are ripe for reaping the benefits of a modern denotational semantics: the semantics of side-effects which govern the interaction of a program with the computer’s memory, and the semantics of concurrent processes.

We describe some basic definitions and results in “wild (incoherent) category theory”, i.e. the study of untruncated category-like objects in univalent foundations that may lack higher coherences and saturation/completeness assumptions. Some of this is based on the Homotopy Type Theory Coq library, as well as ideas from a blog post of Mike Shulman and private conversations with Mike Shulman.
A wild category is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each A,B: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }, a family of types \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )};
for each A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }, an element 1 _{ A } : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , A \right )};

for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} and g: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( B , C \right )}, an element { \left ( f;g \right )} : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , C \right )}

for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )}, a identification \mathsf{lunit} _{ \mathcal{C} } f : { \left ( 1 _{ A } ;f \right )} = f and a identification \mathsf{runit} _{ \mathcal{C} } f : { \left ( f; 1 _{ B } \right )} =f;

for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} and g: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( B , C \right )} and h: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( C , D \right )}, a identification \mathsf{assoc} _{ \mathcal{C} } \, f \, g \, h : f; { \left ( g;h \right )} = { \left ( f;g \right )} ;h.

mainmatter.p: The definitions here are adapted from Shulman from the setting of intensional type theory to an arbitrary wild category.

Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category; a pre-idempotent on an object A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} } is defined to be a morphism f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , A \right )} together with an identification \phi : f;f = f.

Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} } is defined to be a pre-idempotent { \left ( f, \phi \right )} on A together with an identification \chi between f; \phi : f; { \left ( f;f \right )} = f;f and the following composite identification:
mainmatter.tex: f; { \left ( f;f \right )} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{assoc} _{ \mathcal{C} } \, f \, f \, f} { \left ( f;f \right )} ;f \xrightarrow{\phi ;f} f;f

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} and \mathcal{D} be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from \mathcal{C} to \mathcal{D} is given by the following data:

ol.li: for each A,B: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }, a function \mathbf{hom} _{ F } : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} \to \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{D} } { \left ( \mathbf{ob} _{ F } A , \mathbf{ob} _{ F } B \right )}.

mainmatter.p: In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs.

Let \mathcal{C} and \mathcal{D} be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from \mathcal{C} to \mathcal{D} is given by a wild 0-functor F : \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D} equipped with, for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( C , D \right )} and g : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( C , D \right )}, an identification \mathsf{seq} _F \, f \, g : \mathbf{hom} _{ F } { \left ( f;g \right )} = \mathbf{hom} _{ F } {f}; \mathbf{hom} _{ G } {g} .
Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category; the identity 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ \mathcal{C} }} { \mathcal{C} } on \mathcal{C} is defined as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob} _{ 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } } A & : \equiv A \\ \mathbf{hom} _{ 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } } f & : \equiv f \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf{seq} _{F} \, f \, g : \equiv \mathsf{refl}

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} and \mathcal{D} be wild categories, and let D: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{D} } be an object of \mathcal{D}. We define the constant 0-functor on D: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{D} } out of \mathcal{C} to be the 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{\Delta D} { \mathcal{D} } defined as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} \mathbf{ob} _{ \Delta D } C & : \equiv D \\ \mathbf{hom} _{ \Delta D } f & : \equiv 1 _{ D } \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \mathsf{seq} _{F} \, f \, g : \equiv \mathsf{refl}

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} , \mathcal{D} be two wild categories, and let F,G: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D} be two wild 0-functors from \mathcal{C} to \mathcal{D}. A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data:

ol.li: for each A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }, an element \alpha _{A} : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{D} } { \left ( \mathbf{ob} _{ F } A , \mathbf{ob} _{ G } A \right )};
for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )}, an identification \alpha _{f} : \alpha _A; \mathbf{hom} _{ G } {f} = \mathbf{hom} _{ F } {f}; \alpha _B.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} , \mathcal{D} be two wild categories, and let F,G: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D} be two wild 1-semi-functors from \mathcal{C} to \mathcal{D}. A wild semi-coherent natural transformation is defined by the data of a wild natural transformation \alpha : F \to G together with, for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} and g : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( B , C \right )}, a higher identification \mathsf{seq} _{ \alpha } \, f \, g between \alpha _{ f;g } : F { \left ( f;g \right )} ; \alpha _{ C } = \alpha _{ A } ;G { \left ( f;g \right )} and the composite identification:

frontmatter.title: Wild initial objects from universal cones

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category, and let I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} } be an object of \mathcal{C}. If we may equip a wild natural transformation { \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } } from the constant 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{\Delta I} { \mathcal{C} } to the identity 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ \mathcal{C} }} { \mathcal{C} } with an identification c_ \alpha : \alpha _I = 1 _{ I }, then \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )} is contractible for all A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }.

(Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones. It was Mike Shulman who noticed who noticed that this proof also works for the wild/incoherent notions studied here.)

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To show that \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )} is contractible, we choose \alpha _{A} : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )} as the center of contraction. To identify any f : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )} with \alpha _{A}, we use the wild naturality of { \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } }. In particular, we have an identification \alpha _f between \alpha _I;f and 1 _{ I } ; \alpha _A. By our assumption that \alpha _I= 1 _{ I } and using the wild left-unit identifications \lambda f and \lambda \alpha _A, we may identify f with \alpha _A.

frontmatter.title: A wild universal cone from a splitting of the generic element

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category, and let I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} } be an object of \mathcal{C} such that { \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } } is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{\Delta I} { \mathcal{C} } to the identity 0-functor { \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ \mathcal{C} }} { \mathcal{C} } on \mathcal{C} for some I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }. Now let r: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , J \right )} and s: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( J , I \right )} be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \eta : s;r = 1 _{ I }, and let \sigma : r;s= \alpha _{I} be a splitting of \alpha _{I}: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , I \right )}. Then we may define a wild natural transformation { \Delta J } \xrightarrow{\beta} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } } and an identification \chi : \beta _{J} = 1 _{ J }.
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Define the component \beta _{A}: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( J , A \right )} to be the composite s; \alpha _{A}; for the wild naturality structure, we fix f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} to define \beta _{f} : \beta _{A};f = 1 _{ J } ; \beta _{B} as follows:
block.tex: \begin{aligned} \beta _{A};f & = { \left ( s; \alpha _{A} \right )} ;f \\ & = s; { \left ( 1 _{ A } ; \alpha _{B} \right )} \\ & = s; \alpha _{B} \\ & = 1 _{ J } ; { \left ( s; \alpha _{B} \right )} \\ & = 1 _{ J } ; \beta _{B} \end{aligned}
block.p: Next we identify \beta _{J} with 1 _{ J }. By incoherent naturality, we have \alpha _{J};s = \alpha _{I}; thus by our splitting of \alpha _{I} we have \alpha _{J};s = r;s and hence by postcomposing r and canceling via \eta :s;r= 1 _{ I } we conclude that \alpha _J = r. Therefore \beta _J=s; \alpha _J=s;r= 1 _{ J }.

mainmatter.p: Working in univalent foundations, let \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be a pair of not-necessarily-univalent universes such that \mathcal{U} is closed under \mathcal{V}-small products of \mathcal{U}-small types; then the subuniverse inclusion { \mathcal{U} } \xrightarrow{j} { \mathcal{V} } is reflective. This argument will combine ideas due to Awodey, Frey, Speight, and Shulman. For any type A: \mathcal{V}, we will define the reflection of A in \mathcal{U} in several steps.

Let \mathcal{U} be a subuniverse of \mathcal{V} and let A: \mathcal{V} be a type. A reflection algebra X under A in \mathcal{U} is defined to be a type UX: \mathcal{U} together with a function { A } \xrightarrow{\eta _{ X }} { UX }. A homomorphism f of reflection algebras from X to Y is given by a function { UX } \xrightarrow{Uf} { UY } together with a homotopy \eta _{ f } : { \eta _{ Y } } \sim _{ A \to Y } { Uf \circ \eta _X }.
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write X: \mathcal{U} for the underlying type UX of a given reflection algebra X; likewise, we will write f x rather than Uf x.

frontmatter.title: The type of reflection algebras and homomorphisms

mainmatter.p: We will write { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} : \equiv { \textstyle \sum _{ X: \mathcal{U} }} {A \to X} for the type of reflection algebras under A; given two reflection algebras X,Y: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} we will write { \left ( X \multimap Y \right )} : \equiv { \textstyle \sum _{ f:X \to Y }} { \eta _{ Y } } \sim _{ A \to Y } { f \circ \eta _{ X } } for the type of homomorphisms of reflection algebras.

frontmatter.title: Identity and composition of reflection algebra homomorphisms

mainmatter.p: Let X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} be a reflection algebra; we define the identity homomorphism 1 _{ X } :X \multimap X on X as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} 1 _{ X } x : \equiv x \\ \eta _{ 1 _{ X } } a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}
mainmatter.p: Now let Y,Z: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} be two further reflection algebras and let f : X \multimap Y and g : Y \multimap Z be two homomorphisms of reflection algebras. The composite homomorphism f;g : X \multimap Z is defined like so:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} { \left ( f;g \right )} x : \equiv g { \left ( fx \right )} \\ \eta _{ f;g } a : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ g } \, { \left ( \eta _{ f } a \right )} \bullet \eta _{ g } a \end{array}

mainmatter.p: Given a pair of reflection algebras X,Y: { \mathcal{U} } \downarrow{A} and a pair of homomorphisms f,g : X \multimap Y, a homotopy from f to g is defined to be a homotopy \phi : { Uf } \sim _{ X \to Y } { Ug } between the underlying functions together with a witness \bar{\phi} of the following coherence:
We shall write { f } \sim _{ X \multimap Y } { g } for the type of homotopies from the homomorphism f to the homomorphism g.

Given a path of reflection algebras X,Y: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} and a homomorphism f : X \multimap Y, the type { \textstyle \sum _{ g:X \multimap Y }} { { g } \sim _{ X \multimap Y } { f } } of homomorphisms equipped with a homotopy to f is contractible, by function extensionality and the characterization of path spaces in dependent sums. Hence it follows that the canonical map { f } =_{ X \multimap Y } { g } \to { f } \sim _{ X \multimap Y } { g } is an equivalence.

Given a type K and a family of reflection algebras X : K \to { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} such that the dependent product { \textstyle \prod _{ k:K }} U { \left ( Xk \right )} lies in \mathcal{U}, we may define the product of product of reflection algebras { \textstyle \prod _{ k:K }} Xk to have the following structure map:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} \eta _{ { \textstyle \prod _{ k:K }} Xk } : A \to { \textstyle \prod _{ k:K }} {Xk} \\ \eta _{ { \textstyle \prod _{ k:K }} Xk } a : \equiv \lambda k:K. \, \eta _{ Xk } a \end{array}

mainmatter.p: Let X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} be a reflection algebra; a displayed reflection algebra Y over X is defined to be a dependent type \bar{U} Y : X \to \mathcal{U} together with a dependent function \bar{\eta} _{ Y } : { \textstyle \prod _{ a:A }} { \bar{U} Y { \left ( \eta _{ X } a \right )} }.
We will write Y[x] for \bar{U} Yx.

Given a displayed reflection algebra Y over a reflection algebra X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}, we may define the total reflection algebra \widetilde{Y} : { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} of Y as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} U \widetilde{Y} : \equiv { \textstyle \sum _{ x:Ux }} Y[x] \\ \eta _{ \widetilde{Y} } a : \equiv { \left ( \eta _{ X } a, \bar{\eta} _{ Y } a \right )} \end{array}
mainmatter.p: The projection function { U \widetilde{Y} } \xrightarrow{\pi _1} { UX } extends to a homomorphism of reflection algebras \pi _{ Y } : \widetilde{Y} \multimap X as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} \pi _{ Y } { \left ( x,y \right )} : \equiv x \\ \alpha _{ \pi _{ Y } } a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}

frontmatter.title: Displayed reflection algebras over displayed reflection algebras

mainmatter.p: If Y is a displayed reflection algebra over a reflection algebra X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}, we define a displayed reflection algebra over Y to be a displayed reflection algebra in the original sense over the total reflection algebra \widetilde{Y} corresponding to Y.

Letting \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be an impredicative pair of universes with A: \mathcal{V} a type in the outer universe, our goal is to construct a limit cone over the identity functor in the (∞,1)-category { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} of reflection algebras in \mathcal{U} under A: \mathcal{V}, in order to prove that there is an initial reflection algebra. Even though we do not have access to the fully coherent language of (∞,1)-category theory in univalent foundations, we can specify the coherence structure that we need in order to obtain our result.

Our first step is describe a classifying reflection algebra for fully incoherent cones over the identity functor on { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}, which we do by means of a displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures on the product of all reflection algebras.

Let \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be an impredicative universe pair with A: \mathcal{V} a type in the outer universe, so that we may form the product { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X of all reflection algebras in \mathcal{U} under A. To express the data of the components of a given u: { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X being preserved by all homomorphisms and thus representing “cones” over the identity functor on { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}, we define a displayed reflection algebra \mathsf{INatStr} of “incoherent naturality structures” over { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} {X} as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} \mathsf{INatStr} [u] : \equiv { \textstyle \prod _{ { \left \{ X,Y: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \} } }} { \textstyle \prod _{ f : X \multimap Y }} { f { \left ( uX \right )} } =_{ Y } { uY } \\ \bar{\eta} _{ \mathsf{INatStr} } \, a \, f : \equiv \eta _{ f } a \end{array}
mainmatter.p: Given an element u: \widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}}, we will write \epsilon _{ u } : { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X for the first component \pi _{ \mathsf{INatStr} } u and \vartheta _{ u } : \mathsf{INatStr} [ \epsilon _{ u } ] for the second component.
This definition is equivalent to one given by Awodey, Frey, and Speight.

As suggested by the name, the data of an incoherent naturality structure over a family u: { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} UX is not particularly coherent. It will happen that for our goals, we need only add a single dimension of additional coherence data governing the behavior of the naturality structure on binary compositions of homomorphisms. This will give us a classifier for what we shall refer to as “semicoherent” cones over the identity functor on { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}.

Let \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be an impredicative universe pair with A: \mathcal{V} such that \mathsf{INatStr} is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra \mathsf{SemiCoh} over \mathsf{INatStr} of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms.
In particular, we define \mathsf{SemiCoh} over \widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}} as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms:

To define the displayed algebra structure \bar \eta _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} }, we will use the contractibility of singletons several times. In particular, given { \left ( Y,f \right )} where Y: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} and f:X \multimap Y, the algebra component \eta _{ Y } is completely determined by the homotopy \eta _{ f } : { \eta _{ Y } } \sim _{ A \to Y } { f \circ \eta _{ X } }; likewise, by our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the pair { \left ( h, \phi \right )} is uniquely determined. Therefore, we may define \bar \eta _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } to be the unique function satisfying the following equation:
mainmatter.tex: \bar \eta _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } \, a \, { \left ( f, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right )} \, { \left ( g, \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right )} \, { \left ( { \left ( f;g \right )} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right )} , { \left ( \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} , \lambda \_ . \mathsf{refl} \right )} := \mathsf{refl}
mainmatter.p: Given an element u: \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}, we shall write \epsilon _{ u } , \vartheta _{ u } for the components of its underlying incoherent naturality structure \pi _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } u and \varsigma _{ u } : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [ \pi _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } u] for the semicoherence structure itself.
This definition is similar to that of Awodey, Frey, and Speight, except that we do not impose any coherence law for identities.

By the contractibility of singletons and our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the definition of semicoherence structures is equivalent to following more direct formulation that avoids the additional cell \phi:
mainmatter.tex: { \textstyle \prod _{ { \left \{ X,Y,Z: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} \right \} } }} { \textstyle \prod _{ f : X \multimap Y }} { \textstyle \prod _{ g : Y \multimap Z }} \mathsf{ap} _{ g } { \left ( \vartheta _{ u } f \right )} \bullet \vartheta _{ u } g = \vartheta _{ u } { \left ( f;g \right )}
mainmatter.p: Our reason for choosing the more complex definition involving { \left ( h, \phi \right )} is that the extra flexibility comes in handy in our results, as suggested by Michael Shulman in private communication.

frontmatter.title: A generic cone over the identity functor and its generic element

mainmatter.p: Letting I : \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}} be the total algebra of the displayed reflection algebra of semicoherence structures, we may define a dependent function p _{ \bullet } : { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} I \multimap X exhibiting a “incoherent cone” from I onto the identity functor on { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}. In particular, we define the components of p _{ \bullet } as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} p _{ X } \, u : \equiv \epsilon _{ u } X \\ \eta _{ p _{ X } } a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}
mainmatter.p: Above we have used the definition of the structure map on the total reflection algebra as well as that of the displayed structure map on \mathsf{SemiCoh} over \mathsf{INatStr} over { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X. We shall refer to p _{ \bullet } as the generic cone over the identity functor, with the caveat that we have not formally established it as a “cone” of any kind. Furthermore, we shall refer to the component p _{ I } :I \multimap I as the generic element of the generic cone.

frontmatter.title: Quasi-idempotence of the generic element of the generic cone

mainmatter.p: The generic element p _{ I } :I \multimap I of the generic cone p _{ \bullet } : { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} I \multimap X under I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}} can be equipped with the structure of a pre-idempotent. In particular, we may define a homotopy \chi : { p _{ I } ;p _{ I } } \sim _{ I \multimap I } { p _{ I } } using the incoherent naturality structure of any element of I.
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} \chi \, u : \equiv \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I } \\ \bar{\chi} a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}
mainmatter.p: That the higher identification \bar \chi{a} : \eta _{ p _{ I } } a = \eta _{ p _{ I } ;p _{ I } } \bullet \vartheta _{ \eta _{ I } a } p _{ I } may be defined by reflexivity follows from the definition of \eta _{ I } a, the computation of composite homomorphisms, and the fact that \eta _{ p _{ I } } a is also defined by reflexivity; in particular, we compute:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} \eta _{ p _{ I } ;p _{ I } } \bullet \vartheta _{ \eta _{ I } a } p _{ I } & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{ap} _{ p _{ I } } { \left ( \eta _{ p _{ I } } a \right )} \bullet \eta _{ p _{ I } } a \right )} \bullet \eta _{ p _{ I } } a \\ & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right )} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: We will use the semicoherence structure associated to any element of I to exhibit the underlying function p _{ I } :I \to I of the generic element as a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman. In particular, we must exhibit for each u:I an identification \psi : \mathsf{ap} _{ p _{ I } } { \left ( \chi u \right )} = \chi { \left ( p _{ I } u \right )}. Unfolding definitions, this means to identify \mathsf{ap} _{ p _{ I } } { \left ( \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I } \right )} with \vartheta _{ p _{ I } u } p _{ I }.
From the semicoherence structure \varsigma _{ u } : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [ \pi _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } u] we obtain the following identification:
mainmatter.tex: \varsigma _{ u } \, p _{ I } \, p _{ I } \, p _{ I } \, \chi : \mathsf{ap} _{ p _{ I } } { \left ( \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I } \right )} \bullet \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I } = \chi { \left ( \epsilon _{ u } I \right )} \bullet \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I }
mainmatter.p: Canceling \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I }, unfolding the definition of \chi, and recalling that p _{ I } u \equiv \epsilon _{ u } I by definition, we obtain the desired identification \mathsf{ap} _{ p _{ I } } { \left ( \vartheta _{ u } p _{ I } \right )} = \vartheta _{ p _{ I } u } p _{ I }.
Therefore, the underlying function p _{ I } :I \to I has the structure of a quasi-idempotent.

frontmatter.title: Splitting the generic element of the generic cone

mainmatter.p: The underlying function p _{ I } :I \to I of the generic element p _{ I } :I \multimap I of the generic cone p _{ \bullet } : { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} I \multimap X under I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}} has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it.

In particular, the construction of Shulman gives us a type J together with a retraction r : I \to J and a section s : J \to I witnessed by a homotopy \rho : { s;r } \sim _{ J \to J } { 1 _{ J } }, such that r;s \equiv p _{ I } : I \to I definitionally. Our goal is to lift this splitting of the underlying function p _{ I } :I \to I to a splitting of the homomorphism p _{ I } :I \multimap I.

We may equip J with the structure of a reflection algebra by setting \eta _{ J } : \equiv \eta _{ I } ;r, making r : I \multimap J a homomorphism of algebras definitionally, i.e. \eta _{ r } a: \equiv \mathsf{refl}. To exhibit s : J \multimap I as a homomorphism of algebras, we may likewise use reflexivity:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{array} {l} \eta _{ s } : { \eta _{ J } ;s } \sim _{ A \to I } { \eta _{ I } } \\ \eta _{ s } a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}
mainmatter.p: That the above is well-defined can be deduced as follows, using the fact that the splitting of p _{ I } \equiv r;s is definitional and that p _{ I } is a definitional homomorphism:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} s { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )} & \equiv s { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv p _{ I } { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \\ & \equiv \eta _{ I } a \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: We have to check that the homotopy of functions \rho : { s;r } \sim _{ J \to J } { 1 _{ J } } lifts to a homotopy of homomorphisms. In particular, we must construct the coherence \bar \rho specified below:
mainmatter.tex: \bar \rho : { \textstyle \prod _{ a : A }} \eta _{ 1 _{ J } } a = \eta _{ s;r } a \bullet \rho { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )}
mainmatter.p: Note that \eta _{ 1 _{ J } } is reflexivity; we compute the other side of the desired identificiation \bar \rho a by unfolding definitions (e.g. that of identities and compositions of homomorphisms) and using basic path algebra:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} \eta _{ s;r } a \bullet \rho { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )} & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \eta _{ s } a \right )} \bullet \eta _{ r } a \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{ap} _{ r } \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & = \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: Therefore, to construct \bar \rho a is suffices to identify \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} with reflexivity. To do so, we will use an argument suggested by Shulman in private conversation. We recall from Theorem 3.3 of Shulman that the homotopy \rho induces a new witness of pre-idempotency for p _{ I }, namely \chi ' x : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ s } { \left ( \rho { \left ( rx \right )} \right )} and that by Theorem 7.1 of the same, this homotopy may be identified with our original witness \chi : { p _{ I } ;p _{ I } } \sim _{ I \to I } { p _{ I } }. Therefore we have \mathsf{ap} _{ s } { \left ( \rho { \left ( rx \right )} \right )} = \vartheta _{ x } p _{ I }; because s is a section of r, this implies that \rho { \left ( r x \right )} = \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \vartheta _{ x } p _{ I } \right )} and so we may compute our identification \bar \rho \, a as follows:
mainmatter.tex: \begin{aligned} \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} & = \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \vartheta _{ \eta _{ I } a } p _{ I } \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \eta _{ p _{ I } } a \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ r } \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}
mainmatter.p: Above we have used the fact that the coherence datum \eta _{ p _{ I } } a is reflexivity.
Our main theorem then follows from wild categorical considerations, as we show below.

frontmatter.title: The main result: reflectivity of impredicative subuniverses

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be an impredicative pair of universes with A: \mathcal{V}. There we may construct a reflection algebra A _{ \mathcal{U} } : { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} such that for any other reflection algebra X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}, the space of homomorphisms A _{ \mathcal{U} } \multimap X is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone { \Delta I } \xrightarrow{p} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } } over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone { \Delta J } \xrightarrow{q} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } } whose generic element q _{ J } :J \multimap J may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms J \to X is contractible. Therefore, we define A _{ \mathcal{U} } to be the apex J.

mainmatter.p: If \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} is an impredicative pair of universes, then \mathcal{U} is reflective in \mathcal{V}.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each A: \mathcal{V} is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.

frontmatter.title: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

mainmatter.p: I study mathematical foundations of computation, through an algebraic lens. My research spans programming languages, type theory, category theory, logic, semantics, constructive mathematics, and formalisation.

meta.name: venue

link.title: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

meta.link: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

link.title: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

td.link: United States Air Force Office of Scientific Research

mainmatter.p: What does it mean for a piece of software to be correct? There are many possible degrees and dimensions of correctness, e.g. safety, functional correctness, computational complexity, and security. To grapple with this diversity of verification requirements, semanticists develop mathematical models of program behavior that put into relief different aspects of the physical reality of program execution on hardware, just as physicists create many idealized mathematical models to study different aspects of the material reality of the universe.

Mathematical models enable us to reason about program behavior by viewing highly complex objects as being glued together from smaller, simpler objects that are easier to study in isolation. For instance, operational models aim to reduce the behavior of a process to that of individual steps of discrete computation that take place on an idealized computer; in contrast, denotational models reduce the complex global behavior of a process to the simpler local behavior of its constituent subroutines. One advantage of operational methods is that they are applicable even in situations that challenge the modularity of denotational semantics, e.g. where it is not yet understood how to reduce the global behavior of a program to that of its components. On the other hand, denotational methods provide vastly stronger and simpler reasoning principles for program verification when available.

The central thesis of denotational semantics is that programs arrange themselves into geometrical spaces called computational domains, and that a computational process can be thought of as the limit of a sequence of continuous transformations on these domains. Although this thesis has been amply born out for simple kinds of program, today’s most urgent verification requirements pertain to program constructs like concurrency and side effects whose treatment requires the introduction of new kinds of space: for instance, the correct treatment of branching behavior for concurrent processes requires the introduction of higher-dimensional computational domains in which programs can “remember” the specific way that they were glued together.

This project will extend the reach of denotational semantics and its attendant advantages for program verification into terrains where scientists have historically struggled to enact the reduction of global behavior to local behavior, making essential use of new advances in the homotopical and geometrical understanding of computation via higher dimensional category theory and topos theory. I will investigate two areas that are ripe for reaping the benefits of a modern denotational semantics: the semantics of side-effects which govern the interaction of a program with the computer’s memory, and the semantics of concurrent processes.

frontmatter.title: Towards a geometry for syntax
frontmatter.meta: Invited contribution to the proceedings of the Chapman Grothendieck Conference, to appear

mainmatter.p: It often happens that free algebras for a given theory satisfy useful reasoning principles that are not preserved under homomorphisms of algebras, and hence need not hold in an arbitrary algebra. For instance, if M is the free monoid on a set A, then the scalar multiplication function A \times M \to M is injective. Therefore, when reasoning in the formal theory of monoids under A, it is possible to use this injectivity law to make sound deductions even about monoids under A for which scalar multiplication is not injective — a principle known in algebra as the permanence of identity. Properties of this kind are of fundamental practical importance to the logicians and computer scientists who design and implement computerized proof assistants like Lean and Coq, as they enable the formal reductions of equational problems that make type checking tractable.

As type theories have become increasingly more sophisticated, it has become more and more difficult to establish the useful properties of their free models that facilitate effective implementation. These obstructions have facilitated a fruitful return to foundational work in type theory, which has taken on a more geometrical flavor than ever before. Here we expose a modern way to prove a highly non-trivial injectivity law for free models of Martin-Löf type theory, paying special attention to the ways that contemporary methods in type theory have been influenced by three important ideas of the Grothendieck school: the relative point of view, the language of universes, and the recollement of generalized spaces.
